source: Rocky Linux 'About systemd' guide - systemd as PID 1

questions.q: Which SysV runlevel does multi-user.target correspond to?
questions.why: multi-user.target maps to runlevel 3; graphical.target to 5, rescue.target to 1, reboot to 6, poweroff to 0.

questions.q: Which runlevel does graphical.target correspond to?
questions.why: graphical.target = runlevel 5 (GUI); it also pulls in multi-user.target.

questions.q: What may default.target NOT be a symbolic link to?
questions.why: default.target is always a link to multi-user.target or graphical.target - never to halt/poweroff/reboot (that would stop the boot).

options: Checkpoints during startup - certain services/targets must complete before others begin
questions.why: Despite systemd's parallelism, sysinit.target and basic.target are ordered checkpoints; an error there can drop you into emergency mode.

questions.q: Which SysV runlevel is rescue.target (single-user mode) equivalent to?
questions.why: rescue.target = runlevel 1 (single-user, minimal services for repair); emergency.target is even more minimal.

questions.q: systemd must reach which target before it can enter graphical.target?
questions.why: The GUI (graphical.target) builds on top of multi-user.target, so that must be reached first.
